questions.stem: Which of the following best describes culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A group within a society that shares distinctive norms and values while sharing the larger culture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The shared beliefs, values, norms, behaviors, and material objects that characterize a group or society.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The idea that deviance results from the labels society attaches to certain acts and people.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patterns or traits found in all known human cultures.

questions.explanation: Culture is best described as the shared beliefs, values, norms, behaviors, and material objects that characterize a group or society. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: In sociology, the term culture is used to capture which broad idea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A whole group's shared, learned way of life.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A single person's inborn temperament.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A formal law passed by a government.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A biological drive shared by all animals.

hints.rationale: Culture refers broadly to a group's learned, shared way of life, not to individual or biological traits.

hints.prompt: How does a society's overall culture differ from a subculture found within it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The overall culture is shared across the whole society, while a subculture is a smaller group that keeps distinctive norms yet still shares the larger culture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A subculture spans the whole society, while culture applies to just one small group.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are identical terms with no difference in scope.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Culture refers only to physical objects, while a subculture refers only to ideas.

hints.rationale: Culture covers the whole society's way of life, whereas a subculture is a distinctive smaller group within that larger culture.

hints.prompt: Two of the main question's options actually define unrelated concepts. Which pair can you eliminate first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The option about deviance arising from society's labels and the option about traits found in all human cultures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The option about a smaller group within society and the option about a group's shared way of life.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The option about a group's shared way of life and the option about traits found in all cultures.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The option about a smaller group within society and the option about deviance arising from labels.

hints.rationale: The labeling idea and the cultural-universals idea describe different concepts, so they can be ruled out, narrowing the field.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of cultural lag?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A community of skateboarders with their own slang, dress, and unspoken etiquette.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A nation celebrating many groups' festivals and languages rather than promoting a single culture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The same coffee chain and phone brand appearing in cities on every continent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Laws and etiquette failing to keep pace with a newly invented communication technology.

questions.explanation: An example of cultural lag is: laws and etiquette failing to keep pace with a newly invented communication technology. In general, cultural lag refers to the delay between a change in material culture and the adjustment of nonmaterial culture to it. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Cultural lag refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The delay between a change in material culture, such as technology, and society's adjustment of its nonmaterial culture, such as laws and norms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The blending of two cultures that live side by side.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The spread of the same products across the globe.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A small group with its own distinctive style and slang.

hints.rationale: Cultural lag is the gap in time between a material change and the slower adjustment of related norms, values, and laws.

hints.prompt: For a scenario to illustrate cultural lag, which feature must it contain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A new material invention outpacing the norms, laws, or etiquette meant to govern it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two neighboring groups swapping foods and holidays.

choices.label: C
choices.text: One brand appearing in many countries at once.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A group deliberately rejecting mainstream dress and speech.

hints.rationale: Cultural lag requires nonmaterial culture such as rules and etiquette to trail behind a change in material culture.

hints.prompt: Which pair of the main options can be eliminated because they illustrate globalization and multiculturalism instead?

choices.label: A

choices.label: B
choices.text: The new-technology-outpacing-laws scenario and the skateboarder-subculture scenario.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The skateboarder-subculture scenario and the new-technology scenario.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nation-celebrating-many-cultures scenario and the new-technology scenario.

hints.rationale: The global-brand example fits globalization and the many-cultures example fits multiculturalism, so both can be ruled out.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of labeling theory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A teenager acting out more after being repeatedly branded a troublemaker by adults.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two neighboring groups gradually borrowing each other's foods and holidays.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Someone embracing a criminal self-image and reoffending after being publicly labeled.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Withholding judgment of an unfamiliar ritual until one understands its meaning within that culture.

questions.explanation: An example of labeling theory is: a teenager acting out more after being repeatedly branded a troublemaker by adults. In general, labeling theory refers to the idea that deviance results from the labels society attaches to certain acts and people. The other options illustrate different concepts.

choices.text: The Yerkes-Dodson law and allostatic load.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ghrelin and allostatic load.

hints.rationale: Ghrelin is a hunger hormone and self-determination theory is a motivation theory, neither being cumulative bodily wear, so both can be ruled out.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes eustress?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Positive stress that feels motivating and manageable.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The hormone system linking the hypothalamus, pituitary, and adrenal glands in stress.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The view that motivation thrives on autonomy, competence, and relatedness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The idea that one's facial expressions can influence one's emotional experience.

questions.explanation: Eustress is best described as positive stress that feels motivating and manageable. The other options describe different concepts.

hints.prompt: Eustress is a type of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stress.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A gland.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A memory store.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A parenting style.

hints.rationale: Eustress is a form of stress.

hints.prompt: How does eustress differ from distress?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Eustress is the beneficial, energizing form of stress a person feels able to handle, whereas distress is the harmful, overwhelming form.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eustress is always harmful and overwhelming.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eustress occurs only during sleep.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The two are identical.

hints.rationale: Beneficial-and-manageable versus harmful-and-overwhelming is the eustress-versus-distress contrast.

hints.prompt: Two main options define a stress hormone system and a hypothesis about facial expressions, not a positive form of stress. Which pair can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The option about the hormone system linking the hypothalamus, pituitary, and adrenal glands and the option about facial expressions influencing emotional experience.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The option about positive, manageable stress and the option about the hormone system in stress.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The option about autonomy, competence, and relatedness and the option about positive, manageable stress.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The option about facial expressions influencing emotion and the option about positive, manageable stress.

hints.rationale: The hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal definition is the HPA axis and the facial-expression definition is the facial feedback hypothesis, neither being a positive form of stress, so both can be ruled out.
